question:
Why is "saving face" — allowing someone to avoid public embarrassment — often valued in social interaction?
A) It preserves a person's dignity and makes continued cooperation more likely.
B) It is only practiced in East Asian cultures and has no broader relevance.
C) It ensures the embarrassed person will owe you a favor.
D) Public embarrassment is medically dangerous.
